sourceMovie.title: 101 Dalmatians
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: 101 Dalmatians (1996) stuck because it commits to its family premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Stephen Herek's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The family and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: One Hundred and One Dalmatians
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If 101 Dalmatians clicked for you, One Hundred and One Dalmatians (1961) is a strong next stop: both trade in family and comedy, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after 101 Dalmatians?
faq.answer: Try One Hundred and One Dalmatians, Paddington, Mary Poppins Returns—each shares DNA with 101 Dalmatians while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is 101 Dalmatians?
faq.answer: 101 Dalmatians (1996) is best described as Family, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is 101 Dalmatians part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "101 Dalmatians (Live-Action) Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
